metadata.title: Design a LinkedIn Header That Actually Works (1584 × 396)
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4. The LinkedIn header is a billboard with your face punched out of the corner. Get the dimensions wrong and LinkedIn compresses it into a blurry stripe. Put text in the wrong zone and your profile photo covers it on mobile. We are going to build one that works — right dimensions, right safe zone, right content in the right place.

props.segment: Design a LinkedIn Header That Actually Works (1584 × 396)
props.command: Build me a LinkedIn header that passes every technical check.
props.runningText: checking dimensions, safe zone, and brand alignment…
output: 1584 × 396 px · safe zone: right 75% · brand + tagline · export PNG → PDF

beats.narration_text: The required canvas size is 1584 pixels wide by 396 pixels tall. Those are LinkedIn's own recommended header dimensions, and they matter for two reasons. First, if you submit a different size LinkedIn will scale and compress the image, which makes it blurry on high-density displays. Second — and this is the trap most people hit — LinkedIn's profile photo sits in the bottom-left corner of the header and overlaps it. On desktop the photo covers roughly 152 pixels wide. On mobile it can be centered over the left half of the image. So the left 25 percent of your header, roughly the first 400 pixels, is a danger zone. Any name, tagline, or design element you place there will be partially or completely hidden by your own profile photo on at least one device. The safe zone for all text and key visuals is the right 75 percent of the canvas.

artifactLines: Canvas: 1584 × 396 px (LinkedIn's recommended size — do not deviate).
Wrong size → LinkedIn scales and compresses → blurry on high-DPI screens.
Profile photo sits bottom-left, overlapping the header on every device.
Danger zone: left ~25% (first ~400 px) — covered by photo on mobile.
Safe zone: right 75% of the canvas — where all text and key visuals go.
Desktop photo overlap ≈ 152 px wide.  Mobile photo can be centered.

beats.act: set up the canvas with AI
beats.narration_text: Start by generating the background. You can use Canva's AI image generator, Adobe Firefly, or DALL·E — whichever you have access to. The most important thing is to specify the exact pixel dimensions and your brand hex codes in the prompt. Here is the prompt. Fill in your brand values and paste it directly.

props.command: Create a banner image at exactly 1584 × 396 pixels. Background color: [PRIMARY_HEX]. Style: clean, minimal, [BRAND_ADJECTIVE]. No text — I will add text separately in Canva.
output: Background generated at 1584 × 396 — no text, no stock elements
Bring into Canva → add name + tagline in the right 75%

beats.act: apply brand colors, type, and value prop
beats.narration_text: Once you have the background, import it into Canva — the free tier is sufficient. Now you are adding four elements, and all four must come from your brand sheet. One: your name, in your brand font, large, positioned on the right side of the canvas. Two: your value proposition or tagline. Keep it under eight words — this is not a paragraph, it is a label. Three: one visual element from your actual brand — an icon, a shape, or a pattern repeat from your brand system. Not a stock photo of a random city skyline. Stock skylines communicate nothing about you. Four: make sure your accent color appears somewhere, even if just as a rule or a small shape, so the header reads as part of the same visual system as your website.

artifactLines: 1. Your name — brand font, large, right side of canvas.
2. Value prop / tagline — max 8 words, not a sentence.
3. One brand visual element — icon, shape, or pattern from your brand system (not a stock skyline).
4. Accent color present — rule, shape, or highlight so the header matches your site visually.
All four elements come from your A6/A7 brand sheet — no improvised fonts or colors.
props.sparkLine: Name. Tagline. Brand element. Accent. That's four.

beats.act: safe zone and mobile crop check
beats.narration_text: Before you export, do one visual check. Picture a vertical line drawn at roughly the 400-pixel mark from the left edge of the canvas. Everything to the left of that line is the danger zone. Nothing important — no name, no tagline, no icon — should live in that zone. Now picture the mobile crop: LinkedIn can center the profile photo, which means the danger zone widens toward the middle of the image on some phone screens. The safest position for your name and tagline is the right half of the canvas — right 50 percent is bulletproof, right 75 percent is the comfortable minimum. If Canva has a mobile preview option, use it. If not, squint at your design and imagine a circle covering the left quarter.

shot.new_visual_element: LinkedIn header safe zone diagram — danger zone overlay, text placement, mobile crop preview

props.safeZoneLabel: SAFE ZONE — name, tagline, brand element
props.mobileNote: Mobile: photo may center, widening the danger zone
props.sparkLine: Right 75% is safe. Right 50% is bulletproof.

beats.narration_text: When the design looks right, export it as a PNG — not a JPEG. PNG preserves sharp edges on text and logos; JPEG introduces compression artifacts that make crisp type look muddy, and LinkedIn compresses the image further on upload, so you want to start with the cleanest source file you can produce. In Canva: File, then Download, then PNG. Once you have the PNG, open any free PDF tool — Canva's own PDF export, Google Slides, or an online image-to-PDF converter. Insert your header PNG, make it fill the page, and export as PDF. That PDF is the deliverable for the LinkedIn header section of Assignment 8.

artifactLines: Export format: PNG (not JPG — preserves crisp edges on text and logos).
In Canva: File → Download → PNG.
LinkedIn compresses on upload — start with the cleanest source possible.
PDF assembly: Canva PDF export, Google Slides, or any online image-to-PDF tool.
Insert PNG, fill the page, export as PDF — that PDF is your deliverable.
props.sparkLine: PNG out of Canva. PDF for submission.

beats.narration_text: Four traps, each of which will cost you points. Trap one: wrong dimensions. If you submit a header that is not 1584 by 396, LinkedIn scales it, it comes out blurry, and the grader can see immediately that you did not follow the spec. Trap two: text behind the profile photo. A name or tagline sitting in the left 25 percent of the canvas is invisible on mobile. The grader uses a phone. Trap three: a paragraph of text. Nobody reads a paragraph on a LinkedIn header. A tagline is eight words or fewer — a label, not an essay. Trap four: an off-brand AI gradient. When you prompt an image generator without specifying your hex codes, it picks a generic gradient — usually blue-to-purple — that has nothing to do with your brand. Your header should be unmistakably connected to your website and your assignment 6 and 7 brand work.

artifactLines: Trap 1: Wrong dimensions — LinkedIn scales it → blurry → visible to the grader immediately.
Trap 2: Text in the left 25% — profile photo covers it on mobile → invisible.
Trap 3: A paragraph of text — nobody reads it → tagline = 8 words max, not a sentence.
Trap 4: Off-brand AI gradient — without your hex codes the AI picks generic blue-to-purple.
Fix for trap 4: specify [PRIMARY_HEX] and [ACCENT_HEX] in every generation prompt.
props.sparkLine: Dimensions. Safe zone. 8-word max. Your hex codes.

beats.narration_text: The LinkedIn header, recapped. Canvas at 1584 by 396 exactly — no exceptions. The right 75 percent of that canvas is your safe zone: name, tagline, and brand element all go there, because the left 25 percent is covered by your profile photo on at least one device. The value proposition is eight words or fewer. Specify your brand hex codes in every AI generation prompt — if you do not, the tool picks a generic gradient that has nothing to do with your brand. Export as PNG from Canva, not JPEG. Wrap in a PDF for submission.

props.artifactTitle: Design a LinkedIn Header That Actually Works (1584 × 396)
artifactLines: Canvas: 1584 × 396 px exactly (wrong size = LinkedIn scales it → blurry).
Safe zone: right 75% — left 25% is covered by the profile photo on mobile.
Value prop: 8 words or fewer — a label, not a paragraph.
Every AI prompt: specify your exact brand hex codes (PRIMARY_HEX and ACCENT_HEX).
Export: PNG from Canva (not JPG), then wrap in PDF for submission.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Pull up your brand sheet, find your primary hex, your accent hex, your brand font description, and your role. Read the prompt below — fill in your actual values, every bracketed placeholder — then paste it into Canva AI, Adobe Firefly, or DALL·E. Once you have the background, add your name and tagline in the right half of the canvas. Check the safe zone. Export as PNG. Build the PDF. Here is the full header generation prompt, ready to paste.

props.segment: Design a LinkedIn Header That Actually Works (1584 × 396)
props.command: Create a LinkedIn header image at 1584 × 396 pixels. Background: [PRIMARY_HEX]. Accent: [ACCENT_HEX]. Font style: [FONT_DESCRIPTION]. Visual style: [BRAND_ADJECTIVE]. Include: my name [NAME], my role [ROLE], my tagline [TAGLINE — max 8 words]. No text in the left 250 pixels — that's where my profile photo sits. Clean and professional.
props.runningText: building your header in Canva AI — fill every bracket before pasting…

beats.narration_text: Design a LinkedIn Header That Actually Works. Liam, in for Bear.
